---
author: "Jacopo Olivieri"
date: 2024-01-02
editor: source
format: html
number-sections: true
number-depth: 4
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
  fig-width: 9
  fig-asp: 0.618 
  fig-align: center
---

# Sewage Spill Data Exploration {#sec-03-spill-exploration}

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Initialize packages with version control
if (!requireNamespace("renv", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("renv")
}

# Run manual renv commands if needed
# renv::activate() # Activate the project library
# renv::restore()  # Restore the environment if running for first time
# renv::snapshot()  # After adding/updating packages, snapshot the state

# Load required packages
required_packages <- c(
  "rmarkdown",
  "rio",
  "tidyverse",
  "purrr",
  "here",
  "janitor",
  "sf",
  "sp",
  "rnaturalearth",
  "rnaturalearthdata",
  "rnrfa",
  "DescTools",
  "gridExtra",
  "modelsummary",
  "tmap",
  "leaflet",
  "htmltools",
  "servr",
  "viridis",
  "readxl"
)
install_if_missing <- function(packages) {
  new_packages <- packages[!sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)]
  if (length(new_packages) > 0) {
    install.packages(new_packages)
  }
  invisible(sapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
}
install_if_missing(required_packages)


# Initialize logging
log_path <- here::here("output", "log", "spill_data_exploration.log")
if (!dir.exists(dirname(log_path))) {
  dir.create(dirname(log_path), recursive = TRUE)
}
logger::log_appender(logger::appender_file(log_path))
logger::log_layout(logger::layout_glue_colors)

# Ggplot theme
theme_pref <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.title = element_text(
      face = "bold",
      size = 12,
      margin = ggplot2::margin(b = 10, unit = "pt")
    ),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    axis.text = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "gray95"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "gray95"),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 10, r = 10, b = 10, l = 10, unit = "pt")
  )
```

```{css, echo = FALSE}
/* Custom CSS for modelsummary tables */
.table, th, td {
  font-size: 0.9em;  /* Base font size (matches reactable's cell style) */
}

/* Headers */
.table th {
  font-size: 0.95em !important;     /* Header font size */
  white-space: normal !important;  /* Allow header text wrapping */
  padding: 0.25em 0.5em !important;  /* Compact padding */
}

/* Cells */
.table td {
  white-space: nowrap !important;  /* Prevent cell text wrapping */
  padding: 0.25em 0.5em !important;  /* Compact padding */
}
```

```{r load-data}
# Aggregated spill data
dat_yr <- import(
  here::here("data", "processed", "spill_aggregated", "agg_spill_yr.parquet"),
)
dat_mo <- import(
  here::here("data", "processed", "spill_aggregated", "agg_spill_mo.parquet"),
)

# Spill sites
spill_sites <- import(
  here("data", "processed", "unique_spill_sites.parquet"),
  trust = TRUE
)

# UK Map
uk_map <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") %>%
  filter(sovereignt %in% c("United Kingdom"))

# Load MSOA boundaries from local shapefile
msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  here::here("data", "raw", "shapefiles", "MSOA_BCG"),
  quiet = TRUE
) %>%
  filter(str_detect(MSOA21CD, "^E")) %>% # England only
  st_simplify(dTolerance = 200) %>% # Simplify geometry for smaller file size
  select(MSOA21CD, MSOA21NM, geometry) %>%
  rename(msoa_code = MSOA21CD, msoa_name = MSOA21NM) %>%
  # Calculate area in km²
  mutate(msoa_area_km2 = as.numeric(st_area(.)) / 1000000)

# Load MSOA population data
msoa_population <- readxl::read_excel(
  here::here(
    "data",
    "raw",
    "shapefiles",
    "MSOA_population",
    "sapemsoasyoatablefinal.xlsx"
  ),
  sheet = "Mid-2022 MSOA 2021",
  range = "A4:E7268"
) %>%
  select(msoa_code = 3, msoa_population = 5) %>% # Column C and E
  filter(!is.na(msoa_code), !is.na(msoa_population))
```

## What Is a Combined Sewer Overflow and How Does It Work? {#sec-03-01-cso}

A Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO) is a relief point built into older sewerage systems, a legacy of Victorian engineering, where a single pipe carries both household sewage and rainwater runoff. During periods of heavy rainfall, the volume of this mixed wastewater can overwhelm the network's capacity. To prevent sewage from backing up into streets and homes, the CSO acts as an emergency safety valve. Once the flow reaches a specific level, it overtops an internal weir and is discharged, untreated, directly into a nearby river, lake, or the sea. While designed for use only in exceptional storm conditions, the system's function is to prioritise the protection of property from flooding over preventing waterway pollution.

::: {#fig-cso-ex layout-ncol=2}

![](book_figs/cso_ex_02.png){#fig-cso-01}

![](book_figs/cso_ex_03.png){#fig-cso-01}

Examples of CSO
:::

## Location of Unique Sewage Spill Sites {#sec-03-02-spill-locations}

The map below shows all sewage spill sites. These sites represent the infrastructure points where Combined Sewer Overflows can occur during periods of system capacity exceedance.

```{r spill-sites-map}
#| eval: false

# Create function for unique spill sites map
plot_spill_sites_tmap <- function(data) {
  # Convert spill sites to sf object
  sites_sf <- data %>%
    st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700) %>%
    st_transform(crs = 4326)

  # Create interactive map with OpenStreetMap background
  tmap_mode("view")

  tm_basemap("OpenStreetMap") +
    tm_shape(sites_sf) +
    tm_dots(
      size = 0.3,
      fill = "#1f4e79",
      fill_alpha = 0.7,
      popup.vars = c("water_company")
    ) +
    tm_view(
      set_view = c(-2, 53, 6)
    )
}

# Create the map
spill_sites_map <- plot_spill_sites_tmap(spill_sites)
```

```{r save-spill-sites-map}
#| eval: false

# Save the interactive map
path <- here("book", "book_figs", "unique_spill_sites.html")
tmap_save(
  spill_sites_map,
  filename = path,
  selfcontained = TRUE
)
```

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/unique_spill_sites.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

## Geographic Distribution in 2023 {#sec-03-03-geo-distribution}

```{r plot-spill-stats}
# Join yearly spill aggregates with site metadata
# Why: ensure coordinates and company/site attributes are available for spatial aggregation
dat_yr_clean <- left_join(
  select(dat_yr, -ngr),
  spill_sites,
  by = join_by(water_company, site_id)
)

# Choropleth helper for consistent, readable maps
# Why: enforce consistent symbology and avoid re-implementing mapping boilerplate
plot_spill_map_tmap <- function(data, value_col, legend_title) {
  data_for_map <- data %>%
    mutate(
      # Categorise to show zeros distinctly and avoid skewed colour scales
      fill_category = case_when(
        .data[[value_col]] == 0 ~ "No Spills",
        .data[[value_col]] > 0 ~ "Has Spills"
      ),
      # Use continuous scale only for areas with positive values
      fill_value = ifelse(.data[[value_col]] == 0, NA, .data[[value_col]])
    ) %>%
    select(
      msoa_name,
      msoa_code,
      site_count,
      water_companies,
      spill_count_yr,
      spill_hrs_yr,
      spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted,
      spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted,
      log_spill_count_yr,
      log_spill_hrs_yr,
      fill_category,
      fill_value,
      all_of(value_col)
    )

  tm_basemap("CartoDB.Positron") +
    # Layer 1: categorical layer to separate zeros
    tm_shape(data_for_map) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "fill_category",
      fill_alpha = 0.7,
      col = alpha("grey95", 0.3),
      lwd = 0.01,
      fill.scale = tm_scale(
        values = c("No Spills" = "grey90", "Has Spills" = "#440154")
      ),
      fill.legend = tm_legend_hide()
    ) +
    # Layer 2: continuous scale for positive values only
    tm_shape(filter(data_for_map, fill_category == "Has Spills")) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "fill_value",
      fill_alpha = 0.7,
      col = alpha("grey95", 0.3),
      lwd = 0.01,
      fill.scale = tm_scale(values = "-magma", interval = "continuous"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(
        title = legend_title,
        position = c("left", "bottom")
      ),
      popup.vars = c(
        "MSOA Name" = "msoa_name",
        "Number of Sites" = "site_count",
        "Water Companies" = "water_companies",
        "Total Spill Count" = "spill_count_yr",
        "Total Spill Hours" = "spill_hrs_yr",
        "Pop-Density Weighted Spill Count" = "spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted",
        "Pop-Density Weighted Spill Hours" = "spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted"
        #"Log Spill Count" = "log_spill_count_yr",
        #"Log Spill Hours" = "log_spill_hrs_yr"
      )
    ) +
    tm_layout(legend.text.size = 0.8, legend.title.size = 1) +
    tm_view(set_view = c(-2, 53, 5.5))
}

# Aggregate site-level data to MSOA polygons and build comparable metrics
# Why: harmonise spill intensity by area/population to enable like-for-like comparison
aggregate_spills_to_msoa <- function(data, year) {
  # Convert to sf and join to MSOA
  spill_sites_sf <- data %>%
    filter(year == !!year) %>%
    # Winsorize raw site-year inputs once to limit extreme leverage upstream
    # Why: apply a single, transparent outlier treatment before any aggregation/derivation
    mutate(
      across(
        c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ DescTools::Winsorize(
          .x,
          val = stats::quantile(.x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
        )
      )
    ) %>%
    st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700)

  sites_with_msoa <- st_join(spill_sites_sf, msoa_boundaries, join = st_within)

  # Collapse to MSOA with counts and firm coverage
  msoa_aggregated <- sites_with_msoa %>%
    st_drop_geometry() %>%
    filter(!is.na(msoa_code)) %>%
    group_by(msoa_code, msoa_name) %>%
    summarise(
      spill_count_yr = sum(spill_count_yr, na.rm = TRUE),
      spill_hrs_yr = sum(spill_hrs_yr, na.rm = TRUE),
      site_count = n(),
      water_companies = paste(unique(water_company), collapse = ", "),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  # Join to boundaries, fill zeros, compute derived metrics with across
  msoa_with_spills <- msoa_boundaries %>%
    left_join(msoa_population, by = "msoa_code") %>%
    left_join(msoa_aggregated, by = c("msoa_code", "msoa_name")) %>%
    mutate(
      spill_count_yr = replace_na(spill_count_yr, 0),
      spill_hrs_yr = replace_na(spill_hrs_yr, 0),
      site_count = replace_na(site_count, 0),
      water_companies = replace_na(water_companies, "None")
    ) %>%
    # Population density: used as context rather than mapped directly
    mutate(
      pop_density = ifelse(
        msoa_area_km2 > 0,
        msoa_population / msoa_area_km2,
        0
      )
    ) %>%
    # Build area- and population-adjusted metrics compactly
    mutate(
      across(
        c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ .x / msoa_area_km2,
        .names = "{.col}_per_km2"
      ),
      across(
        c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ ifelse(msoa_population > 0, (.x / msoa_population) * 1000, 0),
        .names = "{.col}_per_1000_pop"
      ),
      across(
        c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
        ~ ifelse(
          msoa_population > 0,
          (.x * msoa_area_km2) / msoa_population,
          0
        ),
        .names = "{.col}_pop_density_weighted"
      )
    ) %>%
    # Log-transform for interpretability under skew; use log1p for zeros
    mutate(
      across(
        c(
          spill_count_yr,
          spill_hrs_yr,
          spill_count_yr_per_km2,
          spill_hrs_yr_per_km2,
          spill_count_yr_per_1000_pop,
          spill_hrs_yr_per_1000_pop,
          spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted,
          spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted
        ),
        ~ log1p(.x),
        .names = "log_{.col}"
      )
    ) %>%
    # Transform to WGS84 for web mapping
    st_transform(crs = 4326)

  msoa_with_spills
}
```

```{r yearly-plot-spec}
#| eval: false

process_yearly_data <- function(data, year) aggregate_spills_to_msoa(data, year)

# Create plots for multiple years with a compact spec table
create_yearly_plots <- function(sf_data, year) {
  spec <- tibble::tribble(
    ~key,
    ~var,
    ~title,
    "hrs_tmap",
    "log_spill_hrs_yr",
    "Log Total\nSpill Hours",
    "count_tmap",
    "log_spill_count_yr",
    "Log Total\nSpill Count",
    "hrs_density_tmap",
    "log_spill_hrs_yr_per_km2",
    "Log Spill Hours\nper km²",
    "count_density_tmap",
    "log_spill_count_yr_per_km2",
    "Log Spill Count\nper km²",
    "hrs_percap_tmap",
    "log_spill_hrs_yr_per_1000_pop",
    "Log Spill Hours\nper 1000 Pop",
    "count_percap_tmap",
    "log_spill_count_yr_per_1000_pop",
    "Log Spill Count\nper 1000 Pop",
    "hrs_popdens_tmap",
    "log_spill_hrs_yr_pop_density_weighted",
    "Log Spill Hours\nPop-Density Weighted",
    "count_popdens_tmap",
    "log_spill_count_yr_pop_density_weighted",
    "Log Spill Count\nPop-Density Weighted"
  )

  plots <- purrr::pmap(
    list(spec$var, spec$title),
    ~ plot_spill_map_tmap(sf_data, ..1, ..2)
  )

  rlang::set_names(plots, spec$key)
}
```

```{r yearly-plot-build}
#| eval: false

# Process data and create plots for selected years
years <- c(2023)
plots <- years %>%
  set_names(paste0("dat_sf", str_sub(., 3, 4))) %>%
  map(~ process_yearly_data(dat_yr_clean, .)) %>%
  imap(~ create_yearly_plots(.x, .y))
```

```{r yearly-plot-save}
#| eval: false

# Function to save tmap plots
save_tmap_plots <- function(plot_list, base_name) {
  tmap_mode("view")

  # Save total plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_total_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_total_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )

  # Save density plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_density_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_density_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_density_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_density_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )

  # Save per capita plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_percap_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_percap_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_percap_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_percap_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )

  # Save population-density weighted plots
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$hrs_popdens_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_popdens_hours.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
  tmap_save(
    plot_list$count_popdens_tmap,
    filename = here::here(
      "book",
      "book_figs",
      paste0(base_name, "_popdens_count.html")
    ),
    selfcontained = TRUE
  )
}

# Save all plots for each year
walk2(
  plots,
  names(plots),
  ~ save_tmap_plots(.x, .y)
)
```

### Total Spills {#sec-03-03-01-total}

The maps below display the total number and duration of sewage overflow spills, aggregated at the Middle Layer Super Output Area (MSOA)---a standard statistical geography in England designed to represent areas with roughly similar population sizes (~8000 people or ~3000 households).

Because MSOAs differ considerably in land area, it's difficult to draw conclusions. Even so, sewage spills appear relatively evenly distributed across England. Fewer spill sites are visible around London, though this likely reflects the very small geographic size of MSOAs in high-density urban areas rather than a true absence of spills. There also appears to be heavier spill activitiy in the North and South West.

::: panel-tabset
#### Count

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_sf23_total_count.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

#### Duration

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_sf23_total_hours.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```
:::

### Population-Density Weighted Spills {#sec-03-03-02-popdens}

The maps display the total number and duration of sewage overflows, normalised by residents per square kilometre. Spill intensity appears to be higher in the North and the South West. We cannot determine whether this reflects older sewer infrastructure, higher rainfall, or both.

::: panel-tabset
#### Count

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_sf23_popdens_count.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```

#### Duration

```{=html}
<iframe src="book_figs/dat_sf23_popdens_hours.html" width="800" height="600" frameborder="0"></iframe>
```
:::

## Frequency and Duration of Sewage Spills {#sec-03-04-frequency-duration}

Both the summary tables and the histograms show that the frequency and duration are very positively skewed. A priori, this could be due various factors (also considering that left side is bound at zero):

1.  **Malfunctioning of EDM sensors**: Malfunctioning EDM sensors may detect 'blank' sewage overflows, creating outliers in the right tail.
2.  **Extreme weather events**: Sewage overflows are (theoretically) caused by rainfall. Episodic heavy rain could create temporal clusters of long-duration events, stretching the right tail.
3.  **Aging or Inconsistent Infrastructure**: Older sewer systems or uneven maintenance across regions could lead to chronic sewage spills, stretching the right tail

### Summary Tables {#sec-03-04-01-summary-tables}

```{r build-summary-tables}
dat_yr_clean_list <- map(
  2023:2021,
  function(yr) {
    # Get required variables
    dat_yr_clean %>%
      filter(year == yr) %>%
      select(
        water_company,
        contains("spill"),
        -ends_with("ea"),
        -contains("log")
      ) %>%
      mutate(
        `Sites with Spill Count > 0` = if_else(spill_count_yr > 0, 1, 0),
        `Sites with Spill Duration >= 15m` = if_else(spill_hrs_yr >= 0.25, 1, 0)
      ) %>%
      rename(
        `Spill Count` = spill_count_yr,
        `Spill Duration (hrs)` = spill_hrs_yr,
        `Water Company` = water_company
      )
  }
)
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-summary-2023}
datasummary_skim(dat_yr_clean_list[[1]], fmt = 2)
```

#### 2022

```{r show-summary-2022}
datasummary_skim(dat_yr_clean_list[[2]], fmt = 2)
```

#### 2021

```{r show-summary-2021}
datasummary_skim(dat_yr_clean_list[[3]], fmt = 2)
```

:::

### Histogram in 2023 {#sec-03-04-02-histogram}

```{r hist-duration-2023}
plot_hist <- function(data = dat_yr_clean, yr, x_var, x_label, bins, title) {
  filter(data, year == yr) %>%
    ggplot(., aes(x = {{ x_var }})) +
    geom_histogram(
      bins = bins,
      fill = viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.7,
      col = "black"
    ) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      x = x_label,
      y = "Count"
    ) +
    theme_pref
}

plot_hist_norm <- function(
  data = dat_yr_clean,
  yr,
  x_var,
  x_label,
  bins,
  title
) {
  data <- filter(data, year == yr) %>%
    mutate({{ x_var }} := log(1 + {{ x_var }}))

  mu <- mean(data %>% pull({{ x_var }}), na.rm = TRUE)
  sigma <- sd(data %>% pull({{ x_var }}), na.rm = TRUE)

  ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ x_var }})) +
    geom_histogram(
      aes(y = ..density..),
      bins = bins,
      fill = viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.7,
      col = "black"
    ) +
    stat_function(
      fun = dnorm,
      args = list(mean = mu, sd = sigma),
      color = viridis(3, option = "magma")[2],
      alpha = 0.7,
      linewidth = 0.8
    ) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      x = x_label,
      y = "Density"
    ) +
    theme_pref
}

```

::: panel-tabset
#### Spill Duration

```{r hist-count-2023}
plot_hist(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = spill_hrs_yr,
  x_label = "Spill Hours",
  bins = 50,
  title = "Duration of Sewage Spills of all Water Companies in 2023"
)
```

#### Spill Count

```{r hist-log-duration-2023}
plot_hist(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = spill_count_yr,
  x_label = "Spill Count",
  bins = 60,
  title = "Count of Sewage Spills of all Water Companies"
)
```

#### Log Spill Duration

```{r hist-log-count-2023}
plot_hist_norm(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = spill_hrs_yr,
  x_label = "Spill Hours",
  bins = 50,
  title = "(1+Log) Duration of Sewage Spills of all Water Companies in 2023
  with Normal Curve"
)
```

#### Log Spill Count

```{r}
plot_hist_norm(
  yr = 2023,
  x_var = spill_count_yr,
  x_label = "Spill Count",
  bins = 50,
  title = "(1+Log) (1+Log) Count of Sewage Spills of all Water Companies
  with Normal Curve"
)
```

:::

## Temporal Sewage Spill Distribution {#sec-03-05-temporal}

Sewage Spills are seasonal, peaking between October and February, which aligns with the wettest months of the year. Given this seasonality, the analysis will need to use time fixed effects.

```{r clean-data-mo}
dat_mo_clean <- dat_mo %>%
  filter(spill_hrs_mo >= 0) %>%
  mutate(
    month = factor(month, levels = 1:12, labels = month.abb),
    year = factor(year)
  ) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(
    # Uniform winsorization with yearly data: per-year, 1st/99th pct, hours & counts only
    across(
      c(spill_count_mo, spill_hrs_mo),
      ~ DescTools::Winsorize(
        .x,
        val = stats::quantile(.x, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(year, month) %>%
  summarize(
    spill_hrs_mo = sum(spill_hrs_mo),
    spill_count_mo = sum(spill_count_mo),
    unique_spill_sites = n_distinct(ngr),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    across(starts_with("spill"), ~ log(1 + .), .names = "log_{.col}"),
    across(
      starts_with("spill"),
      ~ . / unique_spill_sites,
      .names = "{.col}_site"
    )
  )
```

### Total Spill Duration and Count {#sec-03-05-01-total}

::: panel-tabset
#### Spill Duration

```{r temporal-hrs-plot}
plot_monthly_bars <- function(data, y_var, title, y_label) {
  ggplot(data, aes(x = month, y = {{ y_var }}, fill = year)) +
    geom_bar(
      stat = "identity",
      position = position_dodge(width = 0.9),
      width = 0.8,
      alpha = 0.9
    ) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma") +
    labs(
      title = title,
      y = y_label,
      x = NULL,
      fill = "Year"
    ) +
    theme_pref +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1)))
}

# For your original spill hours plot
fig23_temporal_hrs <- plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_clean,
  spill_hrs_mo,
  "Monthly Spill Duration by Year",
  "Spill Duration per Month (hrs)"
)

fig23_temporal_hrs
```

#### Spill Count

```{r temporal-count-plot}
fig23_temporal_count <- plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_clean,
  spill_count_mo,
  "Monthly Spill Count by Year",
  "Spill Count per Month"
)

fig23_temporal_count
```

:::

### Per Unique Spill Site {#sec-03-05-02-unique}

::: panel-tabset
#### Spill Duration

```{r temporal-hrs-plot-standardised}
plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_clean,
  spill_hrs_mo_site,
  "Monthly Spill Duration by Year per Unique Active Spill Site",
  "Spill Duration per Month (hrs)"
)
```

#### Spill Count

```{r temporal-count-plot-standardised}
plot_monthly_bars(
  dat_mo_clean,
  spill_count_mo_site,
  "Monthly Spill Count by Year",
  "Spill Count per Month"
)
```

:::

## Sewage Spill Distribution by Water Company {#sec-03-06-company}

The violin plots and bar charts below show the sewage spill count and duration, normalised by the number of sewage sites, for each water company. Certain water companies---particularly South West Water---experience sewage spills more frequently than others.

### Violin Plots {#sec-03-06-01-violin}

```{r build-violin-plots}
# Generate a plot for each year
plots_list_violin <- map(2023:2021, function(yr) {
  # Filter data by year
  data_year <- dat_yr_clean %>%
    filter(year == yr)

  # Reshape data to long format for both spill_count_yr and spill_hrs_yr
  data_long <- data_year %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols = c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
      names_to = "spill_metric",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      spill_metric = recode(
        spill_metric,
        spill_count_yr = "Spill Count",
        spill_hrs_yr = "Spill Duration"
      )
    )

  # Create the violin plot with overlaid boxplots and facets by spill_metric
  ggplot(data_long, aes(x = water_company, y = value, fill = water_company)) +
    geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.7) +
    geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white", outlier.shape = NA) +
    scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "magma") +
    facet_wrap(~spill_metric, scales = "free_y") +
    labs(
      title = paste(
        "Distribution of Sewage Spill Statistics by Water Company in",
        yr
      ),
      x = "Water Company",
      y = "Value"
    ) +
    theme_pref +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
})
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-violin-2023}
plots_list_violin[[1]]
```

#### 2022

```{r show-violin-2022}
plots_list_violin[[2]]
```

#### 2021

```{r show-violin-2021}
plots_list_violin[[3]]
```

:::

### Bar Charts {#sec-03-06-02-bar}

```{r build-bar-plots}
plots_list_bar <- map(2023:2021, function(yr) {
  # Filter data for the specified year
  data_year <- dat_yr_clean %>%
    filter(year == yr)

  # Reshape data to long format and calculate average values by water company and metric
  data_summary <- data_year %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols = c(spill_count_yr, spill_hrs_yr),
      names_to = "spill_metric",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    group_by(water_company, spill_metric) %>%
    summarise(avg_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(
      spill_metric = recode(
        spill_metric,
        spill_count_yr = "Spill Count",
        spill_hrs_yr = "Spill Duration"
      )
    )

  # Create the bar chart with uniform fill colour from viridis magma, alpha 0.7 and black borders
  ggplot(data_summary, aes(x = water_company, y = avg_value)) +
    geom_bar(
      stat = "identity",
      fill = viridis::viridis(1, option = "magma"),
      alpha = 0.7,
      colour = "black",
      position = "dodge"
    ) +
    facet_wrap(~spill_metric, scales = "free_y") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Average Sewage Spill Statistics by Water Company in", yr),
      x = "Water Company",
      y = "Average Value"
    ) +
    theme_pref
})
```

::: panel-tabset
#### 2023

```{r show-bar-2023}
plots_list_bar[[1]]
```

#### 2022

```{r show-bar-2022}
plots_list_bar[[2]]
```

#### 2021

```{r show-bar-2021}
plots_list_bar[[3]]
```

:::


## Temporal Coverage {#sec-03-07-coverage}

Water companies installed EDM sensors at sewage spill sites gradually, resulting in incomplete coverage for some sites between 2021 and 2023. A total of 23,585 observations have full coverage.

```{r build-panel-coverage}
# Create a new variable to indicate complete panel observations
dat_panel <- dat_yr_clean %>%
  mutate(
    panel_complete = if_else(
      available_year_2021 == 1 &
        available_year_2022 == 1 &
        available_year_2023 == 1,
      "Complete",
      "Incomplete"
    )
  )

# Summarise counts and percentages
panel_summary <- dat_panel %>%
  group_by(panel_complete) %>%
  summarise(n = dplyr::n()) %>%
  mutate(percentage = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))

# Display the summary table
knitr::kable(
  panel_summary,
  caption = "Observations by Panel Completeness (2021-2023)",
  col.names = c("Panel Status", "Count", "Percentage (%)")
)
```
